sentences.text: First aware that the world was changing and there was a Hitler and--
In about '35 or '37.
Because lots of German-Jewish refugees used to come from the border to our city, because Hitler kicked them out.
And they were sitting in no-man's-land.
And then, they came to our city and to other cities.
My father also traveled in Germany.
And he used to say that the Germans say, oh, [GERMAN SPEECH].
Horrible times are coming for the eastern Jews, and that everybody was saying that in Germany.
That was what we knew about it, the run in the radio or, or banking or what is happening.
And when the German's started the war in, I think, it was September something, the big--
First.
It, was a one of the-- I don't remember if it was a holiday.
But we were eating our meal.
Well, it was Shabbos.
It was Friday.
Yes, and we have to leave the food on the table and run to the shelter, to the basement.
Yes.
There would be war.
Yes, they said at that time that, that they're just trying.
You know, it's a trial or whatever.
But my father said, you don't trial with Germans.
When Germans start, it's a war.
And their airplanes will come bombing over our city.
They bombed out one the whole street.
And the first, the first man who was killed was a, a brother of my friend.
A piece of shrapnel hit him, you know, from the, from the bombings.
So he was the first--
Romek Zaks-- Romek Z-A-K-S.
Yes.
My father told my mother, look, I will take Rushka-- that's me.
We'll go over the Bug.
We'll go to Lemberg.
I will find the apartment there.
And then I will come back to get the rest of the family.
And my mother said, no.
We all go, or we all stay.
So who do you think won?
My mother.
She said, we don't go.
We stay.
Because she-- actually, she was right.
You don't know what happened in the war.
You want to go.
And the circumstances will be different.
And there, I will be left here with two little children.
So they stayed.
They prepared food.
I mean, they bought coal and potatoes and cabbage.
And we stored in the, in the basements, so we had some [? provisions. ?] And-- The shelter was in the basement?
Yes.
It was-- you see, this was a big apartment building.
And I told you, our [POLISH SPEECH], the tribunal, that even the basements had the walls which were maybe, maybe, 12 inches thick.
So one-- and it was divided.
Every tenant has his own.
And we got ours.
I remember when the-- when we went down to the basement, my mother was so nervous, she just-- kaput!
And I went down with my father.
We, We put a cot in the big room there.
Because my father said, we have to be close.
There is a window.
If something happens-- if the house is on fire-- we break this window out.
And we go out, because we don't want to be burned in the, in the house.
So what I did, we took a cot or two and blankets, find a place in this big room not far from the window.
And then, I was sitting.
It was very-- the kid, the children from the building were crying.
And I, I sat down with them, was telling them stories.
And I had a lots of stories to tell.
First, I knew stories from literature.
And this way, the children quiet down and they listened to the stories.
And they fell asleep.
And that was maybe-- I don't remember how many children, but quite a few.
We were down in the-- we were during the day there.
And during the night, we quietly went up to the apartment.
Because we had to have water.
We have to have some food from the house.
And I remember, the first night, my father went out with me into a little hole in the front door.
And we saw the Germans coming on motorcycles.
Yeah.
No, I think it was the third day since, since they start to bombing-- the third or the sixth.
I-- you know, times are getting--
Yes.
Yes, the first ghetto was in our city.
OK.
The ghetto-- we, we were all allowed, all allowed to live in our apartments.
But the Jews which lived outside the designated ghetto had to move in into this Jewish area.
We lived already in, in the Jewish area.
So we didn't have to move.
My aunt-- two of my aunts from the Warsaw ghetto.
They were smuggled out.
And they came to live with our-- and with us.
And then, there was also a, a, a man who was a convert to Christianity.
And his name was Banash.
And later, I found out that his, his wife was a, a German Volksdeutsche, a German-- and she said like this, listen, my father made this arrangement or my brother-- I don't remember.
We will take Mr.
Banash to hide with us and to stay in our apartment.
And we share with him everything.
And Rushka, Rushka, she's going to run away.
And your place is going to be the place that she's going to be able, you know, like outside the ghetto to be in.
And we gave her plenty of stuff.
We gave her linens, nightgowns, leather that, that we had in stock, just for her to keep it.
We did even give her-- I think, that was Meyer's silver candelabra, you know, for Shabbos six and-- and we gave it all to her.
And I went.
But when the time came to run away before the ghetto was closed-- and it was in October 13 of 1942.
Because till then, we lived more or less regular.
People were taken to the jail.
People were, were being put to Auschwitz.
But life goes on.
And then when, when the, when the-- no, October 13, and I was going to run away to Ostrowiec Swietokrzyski, which was southern Poland.
Because Meyer's relatives had connections there with the farmers there.
And my father said like this, you take your brother.
Take your brother with you.
And you have to take care-- and then, he gave me a whole legacy.
You have to survive the war.
You have to take care of your brother.
And you have to tell what happened to the Jewish people.
I say--
Yeah, see, see, when, when my father said, you go-- because it was already-- we already knew the size of the small ghetto, OK?
So I went there, I went out of the ghetto.
And there were different places.
You know, there was basement under.
And then, when I came out, I was already on the Polish side.
I went to the train station.
And the train was going to leave, like, 8:00 or something, and come to the train station.
And we sit in the train station.
And this is the most dangerous place where you can sit.
They announced that the train is going to leave at 4:30 in the morning.
And 4:30 in the morning-- and where are we going to go?
We didn't have where to-- you cannot stay in the train station.
So when I saw a group of Polish people coming from the train from Radom and I saw a railroad engineer-- he looked to me-- I don't know.
I have the intuition that this would be the right person to go ask.
And I went over to him and I ask him-- with my brother-- do you, do you know where there is a place that we can stay overnight?
Because we looked at all those, those renting houses.
On that street, there were very more places.
But for me and my brother to stay, I would be gladly staying there.
But they didn't have a place for us.
So this man looked at me and looked at my brother and said, did you come from that train?
I, I lied to him.
I said, yes.
So he said, come with me.
And I will let-- I will-- you can stay in my house.
And he took us at night to his place.
And we pretended that, that we don't know that it's not-- we don't know the city.
And he let us sit in his kitchen on the coal something-- box.
And he said, I will wake you up at four-- 4:00, so you will have time to go to the station.
He-- when the time came, he came in the, in the kitchen.
He was already dressed.
And I was sitting with my brother.
I will tell you, this was the most horrible night.
Because we have to figure out what's going to happen, you know?
And he-- I asked him, when the night was over, how much do we owe you?
I wanted to give him 10 zlotys for just let us sit.
And he said like this, you will need the money more than I do.
And he said, I will take you down.
Because I don't want the, the janitors to see you.
Your brother can pass.
But you, he said, you girl, you have guts, is what he told me.
You have guts.
So remember one thing, never go with your brother.
Because one Jew can, can, can mix in.
Two are suspect.
And this was a terrific advice he gave me.
Because my brother had papers on a-- was a different name anyhow.
So he took us down.
And I really kissed his hand.
Because, see, it's not like-- you don't kiss on the face, on the hand.
And he said, may God save you or whatever.
And I went with my brother out to the train station.
And I told my brother, see, this what it is going to happen.
I know that we will be close, five minutes before 12:00.
But somehow, we will survive.
And we will make it.
So we go.
And this is the 13th of--
Yes, of October-- or maybe it's already the next day.
It's already the 14th.
We go to the train station.
And the train comes.
They announce to go to Koluszki, because this is a different place we have to go.
Young Ukrainian, young German soldiers, young Polish something-- they came down the train.
And me and my brother-- I knew how to, to get in the lines and not to be kicked out.
So when I saw of them-- and I was already close.
Because first, they, they emptied the, the train.
And I said, [?
Bennick, ?] you see, we're going back to the ghetto.
Because I faced the murderers.
They were jolly.
And they were happy.
And my brother-- my little brother-- stayed behind me.
And he said, you crazy.
And he pushed me into the train.
And that's how we got in the train.
Yes.
Maybe younger.
Maybe younger.
It was in the beginning of, I think, it was September, right away.
No, September the war started.
Mm-hm.
Yes.
Mm-hm.
OK.
Yes.
First of all, every time we woke up and there was-- the, the Jews have to pay $10,000 or, or to give furs for the German.
They, they knew what to get.
And, and the Jewish Federation, Jewish Community Council, had to come up with all those gifts.
Because otherwise, they took, they took people.
And if we wouldn't have given them, they would have shot those people.
At that time-- yes.
See, the first head of, of the Judenrat was, I think, Tannenberg.
But he was a Bundist.
He was a socialist.
And somehow, his assistant, which was Warszawski was his name.
He took over, this one.
And I don't want to say nothing about Warszawski.
I didn't know.
But the people saw that he really did collaborate with the Germans.
Whatever the Germans said, it was done.
He got to arrested.
And I think-- I don't know.
I know that from those people arrested, one escaped and hid in Czestochowa.
And Tannenberg, maybe he got killed.
But I don't remember exactly.
He was just out of sight.
And then Warszawski was working with them.
Yes.
You have to wore, wore a white armband with a Jewish star.
You were supposed to wear it above the right elbow.
Blue star, and the armband was white.
And I just didn't like that.
So what I-- I very seldom wore-- well, I did wear the armband.
I make a half-shield with a elastic, pushed it down here to the end of the sleeve.
And I put my coat like a cape.
And I put the sleeves in the pockets.
And I was figuring like this, oh!
If a German comes and says, where is your armband?
I'll tell, oh, I'm sorry.
Just moved down.
So this, this how I figured out that I'm going to smart those Germans out.
And other things-- at one time, Jews were not allowed to walk on the sidewalks.
You have seen a German.
You have to go to the street.
And if they wanted to, they would kick you.
Then, there was one in the ghetto, [BACKGROUND NOISE] Willie was his name.
It was a big shepherd dog.
And he used to come to that ghetto.
And he always said-- how did he say?
Hund.
No, he said dog, [GERMAN SPEECH].
Dog, go get the Jew.
And you know what, people-- when he came, it was like a telephone without wires.
Willie is coming with his shepherd dog.
And everybody ran away.
But there was always somebody who couldn't run away, older women and children.
And then, he let them run, you know?
And those children-- and it just bit out pieces from the ankles or whatever.
And when he had enough, then he called back the dog.
So this was a horrible-- Willie was just horrible, a terror of the ghetto.
This was after the ghetto was closed, yeah?
No.
The ghetto was closed in '40-- '42.
This was when the ghetto was open.
What was the ghetto-- the open ghetto?
The main street, like where Meyer aunt lived, was in the Pilsudski Street.
This was a farethrough street that the Poles could go from one end of the city to the other.
This, and this wasn't closed.
And they used to come to buy stuff or to, to, to-- the Jews used to sell them something.
And they--
The Jews didn't go in and out.
The Jews stayed put.
But the Poles, at that time, could come in.
See?
They weren't-- unless-- yeah.
They-- the Jewish [? government ?] Federation had working commands.
And the working commands had to go with a policeman out of the ghetto, always with not-- on one-to-one.
And I remember, my brother was once called to work.
And this was in '39.
And he was, at that time, 13 years old, I think.
Maybe I'm messed up with those dates.
OK.
He was called to work.
And he was sent to work in the-- what's called German soldatenheim, recreation rooms.
They were do a house for the German soldiers.
And when he was called there, he went to send to the station-- to the railroad-- to pick up dust, you know, coal dust?
And they're going to get it through the window in the basement in this recreation house.
And this was, this was a horrible job for a young-- you could-- coal dust is harder than coal.
Because there's no air between.
This is just packed.
So he worked there.
When he came, it was all those things to the soldatenheim.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then came officers and the one who was in charge of it.
And his name was Mr.
Gerhard Vurrer.
And he used to come and said like that, Heil Hitler!
You know, he really gave a big Heil Hitler.
And he showed them around.
And the Jews who were working-- the painters, girls who are washing the floors-- they said, he's such a good man.
Don't listen to him.
He just does it, you know, for, for the game of it.
And he used to say, Heil Hitler, down like that.
And my brother asked if he can work steady in this place.
Because he was a tall boy, even he was 13 years old.
And they-- he-- they would have always taken him to work.
So it was better to have a place to go and work.
And that how Mr.
Vurrer came to our-- into our life.
Because he was bringing food for the-- from the-- for the Jewish people.
They're little, little candies, chocolate.
I remember for my brother, he brought a, he brought underwear, because it was so cold.
And one time, my brother was sick or whatever in the evening.
He didn't go to work.
And then, and he didn't work the next day and, then in the evening, felt better.
You know, maybe it was 24-hour flu or whatever.
And he, he went to a concert that a Jewish, Jewish group-- from children in, in, from the ghetto, and a pianist.
We have the best artists in, in that ghetto.
We have a concert pianist.
I don't remember her name.
She, she was giving concerts on the Polish radio.
And she had to be real, real good to, to give concerts on the Polish radio.
So he went to listen to her and, and some monologues or whatever.
And then we hear a knock at the door.
And it's dark already.
And there, I opened the door.
And there is a German soldier waiting.
And he is, does Benjamin Guterman live here?
I said, yes, he lives here.
And my parents spoke perfect German.
So they took him into the next room, cooked tea.
And I ran to get my brother.
Because how do you explain?
Because for this, he could be shot.
So I went into this show.
And I talked.
And they announced his name, that it emergency.
He should right now come out.
And I waited for him.
And I said listen, there is a soldier in our house waiting.
And, and he's waiting for you.
Mom and Dad just cooking tea for him and talking.
He, he describes what he looks like.
He had a, I think, it was a-- just one little something on his, on his shoulder.
He said, oh, don't worry.
This is that good German.
And we went back.
And this how Mr.
Vurrer start steadily come to our house.
Every Sunday or every second Sunday, he was sitting in the house, singing [GERMAN SPEECH].
Because my father had a beautiful voice.
My brother had a beautiful voice.
Only me was voiceless.
And they were singing.
And when he went to Germany and, you know, he had free time or whatever, he brought Goethe's book with poetry for my mother.
Everyone had a gift.
My brother got a little camera, which looked like a tank.
And I got a raincoat.
And I saw this raincoat.
This, I thought, for sure, it's going to be a terrific disguise.
Because it had this German army color, this green color.
And I said, this would be a good disguise for me.
No, no.
See, what, what he used to say, he had a girlfriend in Kostrzyn, and he-- a Jewish girlfriend.
And he didn't do nothing for her.
And he felt guilty.
I don't know what he could have done.
And when he came to our city, he wanted to make friends with a Jewish family.
And when I remember, I was once sick.
I needed special [INAUDIBLE].
You know, I had, I had a tapeworm.
And he went to Litzmannstadt, over the border and brought it, because I couldn't get it in, in the ghetto.
And then, he also helped our family with marks.
When he, when he had a free hand of buying marks, and in zlotys, he bought the marks.
And we exchanged.
He gave our family the marks on the regular price, like, $2 I think it was a mark or 2.50-- two zlotys.
He didn't want to take one penny more.
And we used to tell him, Mr.
Vurrer, we will sell them on the black market.
We can split the, you know, the profit.
He didn't even want to hear about that.
So-- and then that what happened.
Yes, he survived the war.
He was wounded.
Now, we signed looking for him in Munich, a refugee organization, and nothing came.
One time, I come Rosh Hashanah or was a Shabbos and the telephone rings.
Was my brother.
Was in Chicago.
He said, take a chair and sit down.
He said, you know what, Rushka?
Vurrer found us!
And they way he found us, he found my brother, because I was already married and had a different name.
He went back to [?
Peotgoaf ?] He paid money to the Polish orphanage and said there are-- people have seen them.
I know that they live.
And he, he found my brother.
And then we got in touch with him.
And we used to send him packages, you know?
You--
